overview.body: This is a MASTER week — the final polish on your Module 2 game! You'll expand your function library with input validation functions, wrap your game in a play again loop, and track cumulative session statistics. By the end, your road trip will be bulletproof and endlessly replayable.

mentorQuote.quote: I learned this the hard way — users will type ANYTHING. Letters when you expect numbers, empty strings, even emojis. My first program crashed constantly until I learned input validation. Once you add those while loops that keep asking until they get valid input, your game becomes unbreakable!

objectives: Replace raw menu input with your validation function — no more crashes from bad input
Wrap your game in a sentinel-controlled play again loop
Track cumulative session statistics across multiple games
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapters 4 & 5
programRequirements: get_valid_int() validates integer input within a range
get_yes_no() validates yes/no responses and returns True or False
Menu input must use your validation function
Game must be wrapped in a sentinel-controlled play again loop
Session statistics must track totals across multiple games
overview.filenameNote: Submit two files this week: function_library.py (your expanded module — now 5 functions) and lab-2-3.py (your polished game).
overview.additionalFilesNote: CodeGrade tests your functions in isolation, so both files must be present in your submission.
overview.saveYourCode: Keep both files — in Module 3 you'll add lists to track visited cities and file I/O to save progress. Your function library keeps growing!

rows.task: Wrap game in sentinel loop with get_yes_no()
Track cumulative stats across multiple plays

timeGuide.strategy: Test your validation functions in isolation first! Make sure get_valid_int() and get_yes_no() work perfectly before plugging them into your game.

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here's how your finished program fits together. Checkpoints 1–2 expand function_library.py. Checkpoints 3–5 update lab-2-3.py. The [NEW] and [REPLACED] tags show what changes.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- function_library.py ----
# (From Lab 2.2)
flip_coin()               returns 'HEADS' or 'TAILS'
roll_d20()                returns int 1-20
check_percentage(chance)  returns True/False

# (Added in Lab 2.3)
def get_valid_int(prompt, min_val, max_val):  [NEW-CP1]
    priming read → validation while loop → return
def get_yes_no(prompt):                       [NEW-CP2]
    priming read → validation while loop → return True/False

# ---- lab-2-3.py ----
def main():
│
│── Welcome banner, get player_name
│── games_played = 0                         [NEW-CP5]
│── session_miles = 0                        [NEW-CP5]
│── session_hazards = 0                      [NEW-CP5]
│── keep_playing = True                      [NEW-CP4]
│
│   GAME LOOP:                               [NEW-CP4]
│   while keep_playing:
│   │
│   │── Display destination menu
│   │── choice = get_valid_int(...)          [REPLACED-CP3]
│   │── Destination Gate (coin flip)
│   │── Set distance_remaining, totals
│   │
│   │   TRAVEL LOOP: (unchanged from 2.2)
│   │   while distance_remaining > 0:
│   │   │── Roll D20, travel
│   │   │── Hazard check
│   │   └── (next turn)
│   │
│   │── Print TRIP SUMMARY
│   │── Update session accumulators          [NEW-CP5]
│   │── keep_playing = get_yes_no(...)       [NEW-CP4]
│
│── Print SESSION STATISTICS                 [NEW-CP5]
│── Personalized goodbye

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

content.body: A new function called get_valid_int() that takes a prompt and a valid range, then keeps asking until the user enters a valid number. This is the input validation loop pattern from Chapter 4.6, wrapped in a reusable function!
Right now, if someone enters "6" or "banana" at your menu, your game crashes. Real software never trusts user input. This function catches bad input and keeps asking until the user gets it right.

items: Open your function_library.py from Lab 2.2
Add a comment: # VALIDATION FUNCTIONS (Added in Lab 2.3)
Define get_valid_int(prompt, min_val, max_val) with three parameters and a docstring explaining each
Priming read: value = int(input(prompt))
Validation loop: while value < min_val or value > max_val:
Inside the loop: print an error message, then re-read with value = int(input(prompt))
After the loop: return value

references.description: You practiced the input validation loop — priming read, while loop checks condition, re-read inside. Same pattern here, wrapped in a reusable function!
Shows how to pass values into a function using parameters. Your get_valid_int() uses three parameters the same way.

content.body: Step 1: Add a main() function at the bottom of function_library.py for testing:

content.code:
def main():
    # Test get_valid_int()
    print('Testing get_valid_int()')
    result = get_valid_int('Pick a number (1-5): ', 1, 5)
    print(f'You picked: {result}')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

content.title: Why if __name__ == '__main__'?
content.body: When you run function_library.py directly, main() runs your tests. When your game imports the module, the tests don't run. You can leave this code in permanently — no need to delete it!
Step 2: Run function_library.py directly and test with invalid input:

content.output:
Testing get_valid_int()
Pick a number (1-5): 7
ERROR: Enter a number between 1 and 5.
Pick a number (1-5): 0
ERROR: Enter a number between 1 and 5.
Pick a number (1-5): 3
You picked: 3

content.body: Step 3: Verify these critical behaviors: too high (7) triggers error and re-prompts, too low (0) triggers error and re-prompts, valid (3) is accepted and returned, and the loop exits after valid input.

rows: Logic error: can't be BOTH < min AND > max at once
Returns before validation is complete — defeats the purpose!
NameError: 'value' not defined when loop checks condition

items: function_library.py with 4 functions (3 from 2.2 + get_valid_int())
get_valid_int() keeps asking until input is within range
A main() function with if __name__ == '__main__': guard for local testing

content.body: A function called get_yes_no() that accepts 'y', 'Y', 'n', or 'N' and returns True for yes or False for no.

items: Define get_yes_no(prompt) with one parameter and a docstring
Priming read: answer = input(prompt)
Validation loop: continue while answer is NOT 'y', 'Y', 'n', or 'N'
Inside the loop: print an error message, then re-read
After the loop: return True if answer is 'y' or 'Y', otherwise return False

references.description: Same validation pattern, but checking for specific characters instead of a numeric range.
Shows how to return a value — here you return a boolean (True/False) instead of a number.

content.body: Step 1: Add tests for get_yes_no() to your main() function:

content.code:
def main():
    # Test get_valid_int()
    print('Testing get_valid_int()')
    result = get_valid_int('Pick a number (1-5): ', 1, 5)
    print(f'You picked: {result}')

    # Test get_yes_no()
    print('\nTesting get_yes_no()')
    result = get_yes_no('Play again? (y/n): ')
    print(f'Result: {result}')
    print(f'Type: {type(result)}')

content.body: Step 2: Run and test with various inputs:

content.output:
Testing get_yes_no()
Play again? (y/n): maybe
ERROR: Please enter 'y' or 'n'.
Play again? (y/n): Y
Result: True
Type: <class 'bool'>

content.body: Step 3: Verify: 'maybe' triggers error and re-prompts, 'y' and 'Y' both return True, 'n' and 'N' both return False, and the return type is bool not string.

rows: Using and to check valid options
Always rejects: can't equal 'y' AND 'n' AND 'Y' AND 'N' simultaneously
Rejects valid input — users expect case-insensitivity
Function should return a boolean for use in while conditions
Returns False for 'Y' even though it means yes

items: function_library.py with 5 functions total, all with docstrings
get_yes_no() accepts y/Y/n/N and returns True or False
main() tests both new validation functions

content.body: Your lab-2-3.py with bulletproof menu input. No more crashes from bad choices!

items: Copy your lab-2-2.py and rename it lab-2-3.py
Update the docstring: "Lab 2.3: Road Trip Ready" and update the description
Find: choice = int(input('Enter your choice (1-3): '))
Replace with: choice = function_library.get_valid_int('Enter your choice (1-3): ', 1, 3)
Test with invalid numbers like 0, 5, or 99

references.description: You imported the math module and called math.sqrt(). Same pattern: call function_library.get_valid_int().

content.body: Run your game and try bad input at the menu. Verify this behavior:

content.output:
Where would you like to go?
  1. Corpus Christi - 150 miles (BEACH!)
  2. Houston - 200 miles
  3. Austin - 80 miles

Enter your choice (1-3): 7
ERROR: Enter a number between 1 and 3.
Enter your choice (1-3): 0
ERROR: Enter a number between 1 and 3.
Enter your choice (1-3): 2

Flipping to see if Texas cooperates...

rows: Redundant — function already returns an integer
Accepts invalid choices or rejects valid ones

items: lab-2-3.py with validated menu input
Invalid menu choices no longer crash the game
Game otherwise works exactly like Lab 2.2

content.quote: Before you start this checkpoint, take a breath and look at the big picture. You're about to add a SECOND loop to your program. The travel loop from 2.2 — the one with D20 rolls and hazards — isn't going away. It's going INSIDE a bigger loop. Think of it like this: the outer loop controls the whole road trip. The inner loop controls one leg of the trip. Once you see how they nest, the rest clicks into place.

content.body: A sentinel-controlled loop that wraps your entire game, asking "Play again?" after each trip. Your program now has two loops that work together:

rows: Print title, get player name, initialize totals, set keep_playing = True
Display destination options, use get_valid_int() for selection
Roll D20, move miles, check for hazards — same loop from Lab 2.2
Every turn within each leg (inner loop)
Trip summary, then ask play again using get_yes_no()
Print accumulated stats from ALL legs

content.title: Key insight
content.body: The travel loop from Lab 2.2 is unchanged — it just moves inside the game loop. Dice rolls and hazards happen every turn, on every leg. You are not replacing anything — you are wrapping it.

items: Before the menu, initialize: keep_playing = True
Add while keep_playing: to wrap the game logic
Indent all game code inside the while loop
After the trip summary (inside the loop): keep_playing = function_library.get_yes_no('Play again? (y/n): ')
"Thanks for playing!" message goes outside the loop

references.description: You used a sentinel-controlled loop with keep_going = 'y'. Same pattern here, but using your get_yes_no() function to get a clean boolean sentinel!

rows: Code runs once then exits, or doesn't run at all
Prompt is inside the travel loop instead of after the trip summary
Message appears after every game, not just at the end
Works but redundant — just use while keep_playing:

content.body: Run your game, complete a full trip, and verify play again behavior:
Verify: 'y' starts a new game, 'n' ends with goodbye message, the prompt appears once after the trip summary (not during travel), and goodbye appears only once at the very end.

items: Game repeats on 'y' or 'Y'
Game ends on 'n' or 'N'
"Thanks for playing!" appears only once at the very end

content.body: Session-level accumulators that track totals across ALL games, plus a final "Session Statistics" display after the game loop ends.

references.description: You learned the accumulator pattern — initialize to 0 before the loop, add inside. Same pattern here, but your accumulators span multiple games.

content.quote: Session statistics are your player's trophy case! Show them their total games played, all those miles traveled, every hazard survived. Use their name in the goodbye message — personalization makes users feel valued. This final screen should make them want to come back and beat their own records!

items: Before the play again loop, initialize: games_played = 0, session_miles = 0, session_hazards = 0
Inside the loop, after each trip summary, update the accumulators: add 1 to games_played, add total_miles to session_miles, add total_hazards to session_hazards
After the loop ends, display a "SESSION STATISTICS" block with all three totals
Include the player's name in the final goodbye message

content.body: Play 2 complete games, then quit. Verify session statistics appear:

Verify: Games Played counts total trips, Total Miles is the sum across all games, Total Hazards accumulates across all games, and the player's name appears in the goodbye. Then play 3 games and verify the numbers add up correctly.

rows: Resets to 0 every game — only shows last game's stats
Shows after every game instead of once at the end
Adding miles when game uses total_miles, etc.

items: Session statistics accumulate correctly across multiple games
Final report shows total games, miles, and hazards
Personalized goodbye message with player's name

beforeYouSubmit: function_library.py has a main() with if __name__ == '__main__': guard for testing
lab-2-3.py has updated docstring with name, abc123, section, and description
Session statistics accumulate across multiple games
Session report displays after the game loop ends
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapters 2–5 only. CodeGrade will flag code using techniques from later chapters.

specific: All previous techniques (variables, if/else, loops, functions)
Functions with parameters and return values
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: lists, dictionaries, file I/O, or any Chapter 6+ techniques

finalChecklist.saveYourCode: Keep both files — in Module 3 you'll add lists to track visited cities and file I/O to save progress. Your function library keeps growing!

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read Program 4-1 (commission.py) and Program 4-21 (retail_with_validation.py) — the sentinel loop and input validation patterns are exactly what you need
Test your validation functions in isolation — run function_library.py directly using main()
Check your indentation — the play again loop needs everything indented correctly inside it
Submit to CodeGrade early — it tests function_library.py in isolation, so you'll know immediately if a function is broken
Come to Zoom or office hours — screen share and debug together
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 4 & 5 BookEx Reference Guides — step-by-step breakdowns of every textbook example, your primary reference
